does: This paper reframes jailbreak vulnerability in large language models as a reward misspecification problem during alignment training. The authors introduce ReGap, a metric that quantifies the gap between the intended and actual learned reward function, demonstrating its ability to detect harmful backdoor prompts. They develop ReMiss, an automated red-teaming system that generates adversarial prompts by exploiting this reward misspecification. ReMiss achieves state-of-the-art attack success rates on AdvBench against multiple aligned LLMs while maintaining human-readable prompts, and the generated attacks transfer effectively from open-source models to closed-source systems like GPT-4o.
connects: This work extends the established literature on adversarial attacks against language models and safety alignment failures by proposing a novel theoretical lens grounded in reward modeling. It builds on prior research in reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) and reward specification problems in RL, connecting these insights to the specific vulnerabilities of aligned LLMs. The paper responds to growing concerns about jailbreaking attacks following RLHF alignment and relates to concurrent work on reward hacking and specification gaming in large models. It complements existing red-teaming approaches by providing a mechanistic understanding of why certain prompts succeed in bypassing safety training.
enables: This work opens several future directions for both defensive and offensive research. Understanding jailbreaks through reward misspecification could enable more robust alignment techniques that better capture intended behavior. The ReGap metric might serve as a diagnostic tool for evaluating alignment quality before deployment. The transferability findings suggest potential for developing universal adversarial patterns that expose fundamental alignment weaknesses. Future work could explore whether this framework applies to other domains and whether formal methods for reward specification verification could prevent such misspecification at training time.
